codeReview.summary: Clean, focused security hardening across 3 files (api.ts, use-auth.ts, cors.ts). All three requirements (C8, C9, AUTH-1) are correctly addressed. The implementation uses two-layer SSR protection for refreshPromise, replaces a dual auth source of truth with a single discriminated-union state, and adds CSRF headers on mutating requests with correct CORS support. Backward-compatible return API from useAuth() preserves existing consumer contracts. Code quality is high — no dead code, good comments explaining the 'why', and the discriminated union prevents impossible auth states.

issues.description: Module-level `let refreshPromise` is still declared at file scope. While the 'use client' directive and typeof window guard prevent SSR leakage, the variable remains technically accessible at module scope. This is fine for the current Next.js App Router architecture where 'use client' modules are bundled separately, but worth noting for future maintainers.

PUT method is not covered by the CSRF header check (`POST || PATCH || DELETE`). CORS config allows PUT, and AGENTS.md lists PUT in allowed methods but the CSRF convention only specifies POST/PATCH/DELETE. Currently no PUT endpoints are used in the dashboard, so this is correct per requirements, but if PUT endpoints are added the CSRF check won't cover them.

This API-side CORS change (adding X-Galileo-Client to allowedHeaders) is technically outside the 'frontend-security-hardening' feature scope — it's an API change. The worker correctly identified that this was missed by the prior api-csrf-resolver-conformity feature and fixed it to unblock the frontend CSRF header. Pragmatic decision, but the gap in the prior feature should be noted.

sharedStateObservations.observation: AGENTS.md CSRF convention lists POST, PATCH, DELETE but CORS config allows PUT as a method. If PUT endpoints are added, the CSRF requirement should explicitly cover PUT. Consider updating AGENTS.md to either include PUT in the CSRF-required methods or explicitly document that PUT is exempt.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: apps/api/src/plugins/cors.ts:9 — methods include PUT. AGENTS.md CSRF section lists only POST/PATCH/DELETE. apps/dashboard/src/lib/api.ts:46 — CSRF check only covers POST/PATCH/DELETE.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The frontend-worker SKILL.md does not mention SSR safety patterns for utility modules. The worker had to know independently that 'use client' and typeof window guards are needed for modules with module-level mutable state. This pattern should be documented in the skill's auth flow section.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: SKILL.md section 4 (Auth flow) covers cookie auth but doesn't mention SSR guards. Worker correctly applied 'use client' + typeof window check to api.ts per feature description.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The CORS allowedHeaders gap (X-Galileo-Client missing) should have been caught by the api-csrf-resolver-conformity feature in the api-hardening milestone. This suggests the prior feature's verification steps didn't include a CORS preflight test. Worker's skillFeedback correctly flags this.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Worker skillFeedback: 'The CORS allowedHeaders gap should have been caught by the api-csrf-resolver-conformity feature. Consider adding a CORS preflight test.' Prior cors.ts only had ['Content-Type'] in allowedHeaders.

summary: All three security requirements pass review. C8 (SSR guard): api.ts has both 'use client' directive and typeof window === 'undefined' guard preventing refreshPromise from leaking across SSR requests — all importers are client components. C9 (single auth source): use-auth.ts correctly replaces dual state (separate isLoading useState + useSyncExternalStore with module-level listeners) with a single discriminated-union AuthState — no impossible states, backward-compatible API. AUTH-1 (CSRF header): X-Galileo-Client: 1 is sent on POST/PATCH/DELETE, exempt on GET, and CORS allowedHeaders updated to permit the custom header through preflight. Three non-blocking observations noted: PUT method gap in CSRF coverage, API-side CORS fix outside feature scope, and missing SSR pattern documentation in frontend-worker skill.
